sentences.text: Yes.
She wear a ring, and she took out the ring from the hand, was a diamond ring, and she gave me.
And I should hide it, because we know it already she know.
And I got a bag of sugar.
Everybody got a bag of sugar when-- when we went to Auschwitz, going into the train.
So I throw it in the sugar.
They-- the leaders to the Germans, to get the leaders.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Yes, and my brother and I put it in, and the-- but you went in, you have to take off all the clothes and go like this in the wash room.
This was-- we was also lucky, what happened.
People staying before the crematorium when we arrived early in morning till afternoon.
But was a rumor that we were supposed to go in the crematorium, but they didn't have gas.
The train was bombed, and didn't have gas.
So was already dark, a little darkish.
They took us out, take a shower, and go on the other side.
And we got a pair of pants.
You went naked to take a shower in the same place where the gas comed out.
Yeah.
Was-- they didn't have gas, because the-- the train was bombed.
It was a rumor there, because there we met people, Jewish people, were there from the ghetto that was sent to Auschwitz.
There were the kapos, if you know what the kapos is.
And--
The kapos was all-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were a longer time there.
So there was in the [INAUDIBLE] wise.
They went to work for the Germans, like this--
Yeah.
Was other nations also, but I'm talking about Jewish, because some of friends of my father [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] find somebody what was in the ghetto.
And he was the-- in the concentration was kapo from-- from Lodz.
So he said, one to another, he said they came out to ask [INAUDIBLE].
And also, if I don't mistake, this soldier was watching us, plain soldier, said, they're waiting for the train.
Should come.
And-- and they didn't have gas.
So we took a shower and went out and the other side and went into Auschwitz to the labor camp.
No.
I didn't get a number.
We went into the camp, and a few days later I registered.
My brother register himself, and I registered myself.
And they sending immediately us.
We supposed to go to Flossenburg, another concentration camp.
But on the way, they cut off, and many people, about 400 people, was in the train.
And we went to near [?
Chemnitz ?] to a place Siegmar-Schonau.
Yes, we was a few days in Auschwitz, maybe three four days.
Remember they were just [INAUDIBLE] immediately.
So didn't we have any number.
But we have a number.
No.
We went Auschwitz-- from Auschwitz to there.
We got other clothing.
We got the [INAUDIBLE], this [INAUDIBLE].
Everybody got a-- And with this we went to the-- to the working camp.
Near [?
Chemnitz. ?] They separated the train, and some of our people.
Later they registered the number from here, and this was in Siegmar-Schonau.
This is near [?
Chemnitz. ?] And we went-- we came in around Friday.
So Friday, or Thursday night.
From Friday, Saturday, and Sunday, was the leader, the Oberscharfuhrer, an SS man.
And a whole day make me does gymnastics.
A few days.
Monday morning we went to work.
He wants-- he said this.
He had 70,000 Jews in [?
Ukraine. ?] He killed them all.
And we gonna also come around.
He say [INAUDIBLE] we made gymnastics all day.
And Monday we separate our group should go-- this was Sunday already-- our group to go to work, day shift and night shift.
So I went for the day shift with my brother.
And 12:00, was a siren.
We should get a soup.
Food.
And after the siren, at the same time, start a bigger siren, which they-- they attacked-- planes came.
And they attacked, and they bombed the whole factory.
So they took us out from the factory after it was bombed.
I think one or two was not dead.
He had some hits.
So we went back to this camp where was everything empty, was bombed.
We cleaned up.
They was four barrack.
We make one.
Cleaned up.
The kitchen bombed.
And this Oberscharfuhrer was wounded.
So they took him away.
No.
They took us-- sorry.
They took us to close in the city to a-- a room, one big room.
Factory, but one room.
And they put us in.
They closed us for a few days, without food, without nothing.
And we was laying on the floor, no water, three or four days was it.
On the fifth or the fourth day came in a soldier with a uniform, an SS.
He was going-- he have had flat [INAUDIBLE], short one.
And his name was this, Franz Reber.
I think it is Franz Reber.
And he ask-- he stop and open the door.
He couldn't go in, of course, you know, the smell.
So he closed back the door, and a few minutes later open a door where it was an office.
So he came out a soldier and he said we should arrange five people [INAUDIBLE].
This Oberscharfuhrer wants to talk with them.
So went in a delegation, it's true.
This I know from the delegates, he said who we are.
So they said we are Jews.
So what are you doing here?
We are Jews.
He couldn't get further up.
So in meanwhile he arranged-- this was in the night, about approximately 11:00-- he arranged we should get a soup.
So it came from the military kitchen, came a few kettles, or bottles, how you're saying, [INAUDIBLE] one [INAUDIBLE].
And everybody got a little soup from the same.
One drinked out, we go on the next.
So we got a little soup.
That was, I think, the third day or the fourth day.
And next day in morning, we got something.
And he took out all of us to this place, and we cleaned.
One barrack we cleaned.
And the rest-- we all cleaned all, but one barrack we-- we had [INAUDIBLE].
You know what mean [INAUDIBLE]?
Beds.
Two, three and [INAUDIBLE].
So we took this, everything goes.
And one barrack was not hit.
So we went all laying around the floor, and whatever was to cover, we cover.
Later he arranged covers for everybody.
And we was, oh, staying there.
And every day, after this normalized, we went to work in another city.
We took out machines.

The work was taking out machines from this factory where we worked, what we could, and take it to a little town which this supposed to be a factory of stockings before, which belonged, many years before, to a Jewish family.
This they [INAUDIBLE].
This call Hohenstein-Ernsttal.
[INAUDIBLE].
Machiner. ?] And we built the-- they-- they built, but we helped building, a group of our people, building new barracks.
It took very not long, and we build new barracks and was in this barracks.
And we worked a block, two blocks away in this factory, also made parts to the army, tanks, trucks, whatever it is.
In the 14 or 15th of April, 14 or 15 of April was it.
We-- we are getting the soup in the camp at [INAUDIBLE], and he asked us who has something inside the barrack should take with them.
And we didn't have-- we had-- he was-- you could go talk to him.
But not everybody would stop this SS man, only his uniform.
But we had leaders in this camp, like I showed the-- the picture.
He was very close, I should say, friend with him.
I will show you.
So he was the shoemaker.
And he made them a pair of shoes.
Yeah.
Of course.
Moishe Favel [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Kris.
My wife told you [INAUDIBLE].
His grandson is [INAUDIBLE] in New York.
My wife told you about [INAUDIBLE] in New York, no?
OK.
Can I say?
The Oberscharfuhrer?
Yeah, they spoke every day politics with him.
He was-- this Kris was a good friend of our.
He died in December.
He's supposed to make in March a hundred years.
He died in September in Brazil.
And I spoke with him every day, don't forget my birthday.
I'm sorry I-- OK, so continue this.
We-- oh, we stand-- stand up and we went on the death march.
We didn't know what--
Yes.
Of course, not enough, but we could live on it.
But he, this leader, was stealing for us.
Not he was stealing, he told the people we supposed to get, let me see, 10 sacks of potatoes.
Was-- was laying, prepared.
Once a week we went in a deposit to get food for the-- there were special people from ours went.
He arranged a truck, went with the truck to the deposit, and we got was prepared the rations.
So he told them he should see-- we should see the trucks should be full.
Our people did.
They took a little more potatoes from the other side.
We were steal a little more what it-- what he could, and put up.
And he said you should call me when the truck will be full.
He meant to say will be take out everything, but he knew it.
And so we stole, those people stole, a little more then we supposed to get, thus we had a little more in the soup, a potato more.
He knew it, I'm sure.
I'm not for him, because people after we get the soup-- let me see.
Saturday, Sunday, we didn't work.
Or Sunday didn't work, either.
So he picked up himself the younger people, the older people, should get what is left in the kitchen.
They could throw it away as they wash it out.
They should get a little more soup.
Was naturally left soup, so we had something.
How did you cope with illness?
Was there medicine?
A hospital?
Yeah, we had a doctor in [GERMAN].
This mean not of heavy sickness, because-- but we had a doctor.
French guy which was in camp with us [INAUDIBLE] help, a Polish guy who lived over with us.
I saw him after the war.
I don't remember his name.
He was [INAUDIBLE].
And he was a doctor.
He just graduate as a doctor, or he didn't finish doctor.
No.
No.
In the world, no.
Very little.
We know one-- we was, let me see, in [INAUDIBLE].
We work with other nations which have-- they had more entry to the media.
Holland, Belgium, French people, Pollacks, was working together with us in the same place.
So they tell us some news.
And also, I personally had a German what was living in this little town, but everybody-- an elderly man.
He was 70 something, the high 70s.
He'd bring me-- every day he bring a sandwich.
But he gave me-- he run away.
He said, you open the door will be a package for you.
And he went to the coffee or the toilet, this he didn't give me.
So I got almost every night.
I was working the most night time.
And so it was nice Germans also.
But the media you said.
The media, though, we got only news from those people were the [INAUDIBLE].
They tell us.
We also give over to other people to the-- in the camp.
But he himself, I have to say was a good man.
I was sleeping once, this is nothing to tell.
I want to make faster.
He was--
Yeah.
In the [INAUDIBLE], everybody have to get in.
And he took a few, how you say, [NON-ENGLISH], [INAUDIBLE] was a few which the soldiers put their backs, and they put ammunition on it.
And we start to walk.
And we heard rumors, because everybody had a soldier.
Not everybody, it had soldiers, good one, which has contact with one of us.
So he told this-- they asking the [INAUDIBLE] should permit he has an order to kill us.
And he every day prolonged.
Every day, tomorrow, tomorrow, tomorrow.
Once, he went away to look a place where we gonna sleep over.
He has to ask the farmers they should give us the place where to be there over night, most of the time.
If no, we were sleeping in the-- in the field, and the soldiers were watching us.
Nothing.
We walked the whole day without stop.
And who couldn't make it, so he walked behind.
So the soldiers said, you took rest.
You could rest.
Meanwhile, when we went further, we heard a shot and killed.
This soldier, some of them, went back to our Kommando, how do you say, and some run away.
Some knew it already is [INAUDIBLE].
They didn't come out of here.
So in the end, the end of the day, he was alone.
We knew it is he's supposed to bring us to a camp in Czechoslovakia, Thereseinstadt.
We knew it not officially, Thereseinstadt.
So [INAUDIBLE] went from German to Sudeten, because Thereseinstadt is Sudeten, was a concentration camp.
He couldn't get to [INAUDIBLE], because on on side was the Russian already.
So we-- he knew it.
He had a map always.
And he asked the other officers.
Always passed on the road a car with officers.
So he stopped them and asked them.
So one day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the older soldiers, he-- he was before going.
He was going very fast.
And he went in.
He said, halt.
We went in in the wood, but beginning of the woods on the road is, you know, lower than the-- the road.
So we went in there, and a little woods there, and we saw that he's alone.
No soldier.
This-- this Oberscharfuhrer.
We called him Oberscharfuhrer.
He was the leader.
Left.
Little by little, probably, they had all left.
But every day was less.
But every day, we heard shots.
Who was left never come back to us.
They shot, the soldiers, shot those people what they didn't have-- was elderly people or young [INAUDIBLE] who didn't have the [NON-ENGLISH].
Around 400, or 400 and something, because one person was missing.
He died in the camp.
And he did everything what we wants to bury him.
He was a little older than me.
He came sick, but he didn't-- was always in [? there. ?] And one day he died.
We buried him with the Jewish tradition.
And this, you had to approve it.
We didn't know.
We were further.
We got further.
They throw them in the [INAUDIBLE], you know, they call it the [INAUDIBLE], this--
Ditch, yeah.
They throw them in the ditch, or-- was wartime, and we were-- so he said we going to go to the Americans.
And one side the Russian.
Who wants to go with me could go.
Who wants to-- I haven't got more restriction over you.
You could go wherever you want.
And I going to go to-- he went to the Americans.
And the Americans took him immediately to-- to-- to Germany, in how to call it, is not a concentration camp, it was in a camp.
And they want-- he was an SS man, in the uniform at colonel.
But he was a military man, a colonel.
Colonel.
And-- and I find out after a year-- no, after more than two years, this-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he was liberated.
And he have to go to court, because he wants to show that he was not a Nazi.
So I was called.
I went.
This was in [INAUDIBLE], I told you.
Now I remember [INAUDIBLE].
And there was a lot of in our camp, and what we could say?
So it cames he had the speech.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So he said he-- he never hit the men of us.
He could ask our people to the judge, to the jury, and he wouldn't lie.
Meanwhile he was 2 years or 2 and 1/2 years in [INAUDIBLE] jail in till [INAUDIBLE].
And I wouldn't like my children should go up and say their father's a murderer.
I knew it in the end, they didn't call me as-- as a witness.
I registered myself as a witness.
I-- I have to register.
I got it from the police.
And they pay me even for the far to going there in two days.
Of course after the war.
It was-- the war ended for five-- must have been '48.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Why?
Because he was sitting in jail three years already there, and he's supposed to get 2 and 1/2 year, I think, jail, because he had a-- I don't know exactly why.
And he was liberated.
And this friend, what you would have here, saw him after the war.
And he helped him.
He came to him.
He said, he is-- he was-- really was he a dental technician.
But everybody has to go in the army.
And he was-- he had some with the [INAUDIBLE].
He didn't go straight with the [INAUDIBLE], but they took everybody.
So I know this Kris saw him, helped him.
He was crying, he hasn't got-- he haven't got somewhere to live.
And he's-- he never hit one of the-- of our people.
And this is true.
